title: Clay Army Rises from the Ground
story: Chinese farmers were digging a well near Xi'an on March 29th, 1974, when their shovels hit something hard and strange. Instead of water, they found pieces of clay that looked like broken pottery. But when archaeologists rushed to the scene, they uncovered one of the most incredible discoveries in history — an entire army made of clay! Over 8,000 life-sized warrior statues had been buried underground for more than 2,000 years, standing guard over the tomb of Qin Shi Huang, China's first emperor. Each clay soldier was unique, with different faces, hairstyles, and armor. The emperor wanted this terracotta army to protect him in the afterlife, creating what might be the world's most amazing underground surprise.
activity_question: What were the Chinese farmers originally trying to dig when they found the Terracotta Army?
activity_choices: A tunnel to their neighbor's farm
A hole to plant a giant tree
bonus_fact: Each clay warrior's face is different — no two soldiers look exactly the same!

title: Space Express: Record-Breaking Rocket Ride
story: Three astronauts buckled up for the fastest trip to space ever attempted on March 28th, 2013! The Soyuz TMA-08M spacecraft blasted off from Earth with a bold mission: reach the International Space Station in just six hours instead of the usual two days. This wasn't just any regular space flight — it was like switching from a slow bus to a rocket-powered express train! The crew zoomed through space at incredible speeds, racing against the clock as they orbited Earth multiple times. When they finally docked with the space station that same day, they had shattered the previous record and proven that space travel could be much faster than anyone thought possible.
activity_question: How long did it normally take spacecraft to reach the International Space Station before this record?
bonus_fact: The crew orbited Earth four times during their speedy six-hour journey!

title: Giant Ship Gets Unstuck!
story: The massive container ship Ever Given finally broke free from the Suez Canal on March 29th, 2021, after being stuck sideways for nearly a week! This wasn't just any traffic jam — the gigantic vessel had completely blocked one of the world's busiest shipping routes, creating the most expensive traffic backup in history. At least 369 ships were trapped waiting to pass through, carrying everything from oil to toilet paper. The ship was longer than the Empire State Building is tall, and it took tugboats, excavators, and even the high tide to finally wiggle it loose. When it moved again, people around the world cheered!
activity_question: How many ships were waiting to pass through the Suez Canal while Ever Given was stuck?
bonus_fact: The Ever Given is so huge it can carry over 20,000 shipping containers!

title: Pluto's Ice Volcanoes Discovered!
story: Scientists made an incredible discovery about Pluto in 2022 when they took a closer look at old footage from NASA's New Horizons spacecraft! The tiny dwarf planet, which is so far away it takes sunlight over five hours to reach it, has something amazing on its surface: cryovolcanoes. These aren't regular volcanoes that spew hot lava - they're ice volcanoes that shoot out freezing cold "lava" made of water, nitrogen, and other frozen materials. The researchers found this evidence by studying photos taken back in 2015 when New Horizons flew past Pluto. Instead of the dead, boring rock everyone expected, Pluto turned out to have active geology with these icy eruptions reshaping its surface!
activity_question: What makes cryovolcanoes different from regular volcanoes?
activity_choices: They shoot out freezing ice lava instead of hot lava
They're much bigger than regular volcanoes
They're made of cheese like the moon
activity_answer: They shoot out freezing ice lava instead of hot lava
bonus_fact: Pluto's ice volcanoes might be taller than any mountain on Earth!

title: American Horse Conquers Dubai's Desert
story: Animal Kingdom galloped into history on March 30th, 2013, when this amazing American racehorse thundered across the finish line first at the Dubai World Cup! This wasn't just any horse race – it was one of the richest and most exciting races in the world, held in the middle of the desert in Dubai. Animal Kingdom had to beat the fastest horses from around the globe, and he did it with style. The victory was extra special because it proved that American horses could compete with the best international champions. As dust settled on the track that day, Animal Kingdom had earned his owners a whopping $6 million prize and brought serious bragging rights back to America.
activity_question: Where did Animal Kingdom win the 2013 World Cup race?
bonus_fact: The Dubai World Cup is run on a special artificial surface called Tapeta.

title: The Great One's Incredible Assist Record
story: Wayne Gretzky was already known as "The Great One" when he did something absolutely mind-blowing on March 29th, 1985. The hockey superstar didn't just break someone else's record that night — he shattered his own! Gretzky scored his 126th assist of the season, which meant he had broken his previous record of 125 assists that he had set just two years earlier. An assist is when you help a teammate score a goal, and most players are thrilled to get even 50 assists in a whole season. But Gretzky was so good at seeing the ice and setting up perfect passes that he nearly tripled what most players could do. This incredible moment showed why many people still call him the greatest hockey player who ever lived.
activity_question: How many assists did Wayne Gretzky get to break his own record in 1985?
bonus_fact: Gretzky ended that season with 163 assists — a record that still stands today!

title: Three Swings, Three Home Runs!
story: Baseball fans couldn't believe their eyes when the New York Yankees stepped up to bat in 2025! Paul Goldschmidt grabbed his bat and smacked the very first pitch from Milwaukee Brewers pitcher Nester Cortes straight over the fence for a home run. Then Cody Bellinger did the exact same thing on the second pitch. But wait, it gets even crazier - Aaron Judge crushed the third pitch for another home run! Three batters, three pitches, three home runs in a row. The Yankees were just getting started though, because they ended up hitting a franchise record nine home runs total that day, winning the game 20-9 at Yankee Stadium. Talk about starting with a bang!
activity_question: How many home runs did the Yankees hit in total during this record-breaking game?
bonus_fact: The Yankees scored 20 runs, which is like scoring 3 touchdowns in football!

title: A Shot at Protection
story: Scientists made a huge announcement on this day in 1971 that would protect millions of kids around the world. They had finally developed a vaccine for serum hepatitis, a serious liver disease that was making children very sick. Hepatitis is a tricky virus that attacks your liver, the organ that helps clean your blood and digest food. Before this vaccine, kids could catch this disease from contaminated blood or medical equipment, and it would make them feel terrible for weeks or even months. The new vaccine worked like a superhero training program for your immune system, teaching it how to fight off the hepatitis virus before it could cause harm. This breakthrough meant that children everywhere could get a simple shot to stay healthy and avoid a really nasty illness.
activity_question: What organ does hepatitis attack in your body?
follow_up_question: How do you think vaccines help your body learn to fight diseases?

title: The Day Niagara Falls Went Silent
story: Water stopped thundering over Niagara Falls completely, leaving visitors staring at empty cliffs where millions of gallons usually crashed down every minute. On March 29th, 1848, something incredible happened that hadn't occurred in thousands of years — the mighty falls went totally silent for 30 whole hours! The culprit was a massive ice jam that had formed upstream in the Niagara River, acting like a giant cork in a bottle. Chunks of ice piled up so high and thick that they completely blocked the water from flowing toward the falls. People walked out onto the dry riverbed where the falls normally roared, picking up fish and old artifacts. It was so quiet and strange that many thought the world was ending, until the ice finally broke apart and the water came rushing back with a tremendous roar.
activity_question: What caused Niagara Falls to stop flowing in 1848?
activity_choices: An ice jam upstream in the river
People built a wall to stop it
activity_answer: An ice jam upstream in the river
bonus_fact: People found old guns and tomahawks on the dry riverbed that day!

title: Girl Power Takes Flight on Skis
story: Tiina Lehtola from Finland made history soar through the air on this March day back in 1981! She launched herself off a ski jump and flew an incredible 110 meters before landing safely in the snow. That's longer than a football field! What made this jump extra special was that Tiina smashed the women's ski jumping world record, proving that girls could fly just as far as anyone else. Her amazing leap showed the world that female ski jumpers deserved to compete alongside the boys, and her record inspired countless other girls to strap on skis and reach for the sky.
activity_question: How far did Tiina Lehtola jump to set her world record?
bonus_fact: Women weren't allowed in Olympic ski jumping until 2014!

title: First Photos from Mercury's Hot Neighborhood
story: A brave little spacecraft named Mariner 10 made history on March 29th, 1974, when it became the first visitor to snap photos of Mercury, the sun's closest neighbor. Flying at incredible speeds through space, this robot photographer zoomed past the scorching hot planet and beamed back amazing pictures that no human had ever seen before. Scientists on Earth were thrilled to finally get a close look at Mercury's cratered surface, which looks a lot like our moon but gets hot enough to melt lead during the day!
activity_question: What was special about Mariner 10's mission to Mercury in 1974?
activity_choices: It was the first spacecraft to take photos of Mercury
It landed on Mercury and collected rocks
It discovered that Mercury was made of cheese
It brought back alien samples from Mercury
activity_answer: It was the first spacecraft to take photos of Mercury
bonus_fact: Mercury is so close to the sun that a year there lasts only 88 Earth days!

title: Racing Into History at 203 MPH
story: Henry Segrave gripped the steering wheel of his sleek Sunbeam race car and prepared to make history on March 29th, 1927. The sandy beaches of Daytona stretched ahead like a natural racetrack, perfect for his daring attempt. When Segrave hit the gas, his car roared forward faster than any human had ever traveled on land before. The speedometer climbed higher and higher until it reached an incredible 203.79 miles per hour! That's faster than most cars can go today on highways. Segrave had just shattered the world land speed record, proving that with enough courage and engineering skill, humans could push the boundaries of what seemed possible.
activity_question: What speed did Henry Segrave reach in his record-breaking run?
bonus_fact: Daytona's hard-packed sand made it perfect for early speed records before racetracks existed.

title: Cricket's Most Crushing Victory Ever
story: Cricket fans couldn't believe their eyes when Delhi absolutely demolished Karnataka in the Ranji Trophy final on this date in 1979. The Ranji Trophy is India's biggest cricket tournament, where states compete for the ultimate prize. But nobody expected what happened next — Delhi didn't just win, they crushed Karnataka by a whopping 399 runs! That's like winning a race by almost four laps. It was one of the most lopsided victories in cricket history, and Delhi players probably couldn't stop grinning for weeks.
activity_question: By how many runs did Delhi beat Karnataka in the 1979 Ranji Trophy final?
bonus_fact: The Ranji Trophy is named after a famous Indian prince who played cricket.

title: Ice Dancing Champions Without a Country
story: Two Russian ice dancers found themselves in a very strange situation on March 29th, 1992, when they won the World Ice Dance Championship in Oakland, California. Marina Klimova and Sergei Ponomarenko were incredible on the ice, gliding and spinning their way to victory with perfect grace. But here's the weird part - they couldn't compete for Russia because the Soviet Union had just broken apart! Instead, they had to represent something called the CIS (Commonwealth of Independent States), which was basically a temporary team name for athletes from the former Soviet countries. Imagine winning gold but not knowing exactly which flag would be raised for you!
activity_question: What did CIS stand for when Klimova and Ponomarenko won their championship?
follow_up_question: How do you think it would feel to win a world championship but not be sure which country you're representing?

title: Atlanta's Brand New Baseball Home
story: The Atlanta Braves had a big problem in 1997 - they needed a new stadium! Their old one was getting torn down after the Olympics. So on March 29th that year, something exciting happened: the very first baseball game at their shiny new Turner Field. It was just a practice game against the famous New York Yankees, but the Braves wanted to make a good first impression in their new home. And they did! The Braves won 2-0, which was pretty awesome for a team playing in a brand new ballpark. The players got to run around the bases, the fans got to cheer from new seats, and everyone got to see what would become the Braves' home for many years.
activity_question: What was special about the baseball game on March 29th, 1997?
activity_choices: It was the first game ever played at Turner Field
It was played in the snow
activity_answer: It was the first game ever played at Turner Field
bonus_fact: Turner Field was built using parts of the 1996 Olympics stadium!

title: Australia's Cricket Championship Victory
story: Cricket fans around the world held their breath as Australia faced New Zealand in the Cricket World Cup final at Melbourne's famous MCG stadium on March 29th, 2015. The Aussies were playing for their fourth world championship title on home soil, with thousands of cheering fans packed into the massive stadium. Australia's bowlers struck first, with James Faulkner taking three crucial wickets for just 36 runs to slow down New Zealand's batting. Then Australia's batters stepped up big time, smashing the winning runs with 101 balls still left to play - that's like finishing a race way ahead of everyone else!
activity_question: How many Cricket World Cup titles had Australia won after this victory?
bonus_fact: The MCG stadium can hold over 100,000 people - that's like a small city!

title: A Monument Years in the Making
story: Abraham Lincoln had been gone for two years when Congress made a big decision on this day in 1867 — they voted to build a special monument to honor the president who had saved the nation. But here's the crazy part: it took 55 more years before the Lincoln Memorial we know today was actually finished! The lawmakers wanted to create something amazing, but they couldn't agree on what it should look like or where to put it. Some wanted a simple statue, others dreamed of a grand temple with giant columns.
activity_question: How long did it take from when Congress first approved the Lincoln Memorial until it was actually finished?
bonus_fact: The Lincoln Memorial's statue of Lincoln is 19 feet tall and weighs 175 tons!

title: Rocket Dreams Take Flight
story: A homemade rocket blasted off from the desert on March 29th, 1989, making history as the first private commercial rocket to reach space and come back down. At White Sands Missile Range in New Mexico, this wasn't built by NASA or the government — regular people with big dreams had created their own spacecraft! The rocket shot up into the blackness above Earth's atmosphere, then safely returned in what's called a suborbital flight. It proved that someday, private companies might take people to space too.
activity_question: What made this 1989 rocket launch special?
activity_choices: It was the first private commercial rocket to make a suborbital flight
It was powered by chocolate chip cookies
It was built by NASA scientists
activity_answer: It was the first private commercial rocket to make a suborbital flight
follow_up_question: What do you think private space companies might accomplish next?

title: Ice Dancing Gold from a Vanished Country
story: Two figure skaters made history on March 29th, 1992, but they competed for a country that was disappearing! Natalia Mishkutenok and Artur Dmitriev glided across the ice in Oakland, California, spinning and leaping their way to victory at the Ice Pairs Championship. The twist? They represented the CIS - the Commonwealth of Independent States - which was what remained after the Soviet Union broke apart just months earlier. While their homeland was changing forever, these talented athletes proved that some things, like perfect teamwork on ice, stay beautiful no matter what's happening in the world around them.
activity_question: What did CIS stand for when Mishkutenok and Dmitriev won their championship?
bonus_fact: The CIS only lasted about one year as a competing nation in sports!

title: Antarctica's Hidden Ocean Highway in Danger
story: Deep beneath Antarctica's icy waters, invisible rivers carry nutrients around the world like a giant underwater highway. Scientists discovered in 2023 that this amazing system could break down by almost half before 2050. These special ocean currents work like conveyor belts, moving cold, nutrient-rich water from the bottom of the sea up to the surface and around the globe. When they slow down or stop, it's like turning off a massive refrigerator that helps keep our planet cool. Without these currents, ice melts faster and oceans get warmer everywhere.
activity_question: What do the deep Antarctic ocean currents do?
activity_choices: Carry nutrients around the world like underwater highways
Create icebergs that float to other countries
Make the water taste better for penguins
activity_answer: Carry nutrients around the world like underwater highways
follow_up_question: What other invisible systems in nature help keep our planet healthy?

title: Queen Victoria's Grand Opening
story: Queen Victoria had a big problem on March 29th, 1871 - she was supposed to open a brand new concert hall in London, but she was still sad about her husband Albert's death ten years earlier. The Royal Albert Hall was actually named after him! When the Queen stood up to give her speech that day, something amazing happened. She got so emotional thinking about Albert that she couldn't finish her words. The crowd understood and cheered anyway, officially opening one of the world's most famous music venues.
activity_question: What happened when Queen Victoria tried to give her opening speech?
activity_choices: She couldn't finish because she got too emotional
She sang a song instead of speaking
She forgot all her words completely
She spoke for three hours straight
activity_answer: She couldn't finish because she got too emotional
follow_up_question: Why do you think they named the hall after Prince Albert?

title: Lightning-Fast Champion Keeps His Crown
story: Joe Louis was already the heavyweight boxing champion when he stepped into the ring on this date in 1940, but Johnny Paycheck thought he could take the title away. Big mistake! Louis was nicknamed the "Brown Bomber" because his punches were so powerful and fast. In just two rounds, Louis knocked out Paycheck and kept his heavyweight crown. The fight was over so quickly that some fans probably didn't even finish their popcorn! Louis was one of the greatest boxers ever, and this lightning-fast victory showed exactly why he held the title for so long.
activity_question: What was Joe Louis's famous nickname?
bonus_fact: Joe Louis held the heavyweight title for 12 years, longer than any other heavyweight champion!

title: The Toughest Man Ali Never Knocked Down
story: Muhammad Ali threw everything he had at George Chuvalo, but the tough Canadian boxer just wouldn't fall! Back on March 29th, 1966, these two heavyweight champions battled for 15 brutal rounds in Toronto. Ali was defending his world heavyweight title, dancing around the ring with his famous footwork. Chuvalo was known as one of the hardest fighters to knock out in boxing history. For 15 rounds, Ali punched and jabbed, but Chuvalo kept standing up and fighting back. When the final bell rang, Ali won by decision, but Chuvalo had done something amazing - he became one of the few boxers Ali could never knock down!
activity_question: How many rounds did Ali and Chuvalo fight?
bonus_fact: Chuvalo was never knocked down in his entire 93-fight professional career!

title: Vikings Demand Paris Ransom!
story: Viking longships filled the Seine River on March 29th, 845, as fierce warriors led by the legendary Ragnar Lodbrok attacked Paris! The Vikings weren't just raiding for treasure this time—they had a clever plan. After sacking the city and showing their strength, Ragnar made the French king an offer he couldn't refuse: pay us a massive ransom of silver, and we'll sail away peacefully. The terrified king agreed, handing over thousands of pounds of precious silver to make the Vikings leave. It worked perfectly, and Ragnar's crew sailed home rich and famous!
activity_question: What did Ragnar Lodbrok demand from the French king after attacking Paris?
activity_choices: A year's supply of French cheese
Half of all the land in France
bonus_fact: Ragnar's successful ransom trick inspired other Viking leaders to try the same strategy!

title: The Amazing Parodi Twins
story: Two very special sisters named Ritta and Christina Parodi were born in the town of Sassare on the island of Sardinia on this date in 1829. These remarkable twins were what doctors call conjoined twins, meaning they were physically connected when they were born. Back in the 1800s, people often called conjoined twins "Siamese twins" after two famous brothers from Thailand. The Parodi sisters lived their entire lives together, sharing everything and facing the world as a team until they passed away later that same year.
activity_question: What were Ritta and Christina Parodi?
bonus_fact: The term "Siamese twins" came from famous conjoined brothers Chang and Eng from Thailand.

title: The Greatest Racehorse Ever Born
story: March 29th, 1917 brought something special to a Kentucky horse farm — the birth of a colt who would become racing's biggest superstar. Man o' War was born that day at Nursery Stud, and this wasn't just any ordinary foal. He grew up to be so fast and powerful that people called him "Big Red" because of his massive size and reddish coat. In 1920, he won both the Preakness Stakes and Belmont Stakes, two of horse racing's most important races. He was so good that he only lost one race in his entire career!
activity_question: What nickname did people give Man o' War because of his size and color?
bonus_fact: Man o' War only lost one race out of 21 — and that was because of a bad start!

title: Baby Astronaut Takes First Breath
story: A future space explorer took his very first breath on March 29th, 1965, when William Oefelein was born in Fort Belvoir, Virginia. Little did anyone know that this tiny baby would grow up to zoom around Earth at 17,500 miles per hour! Years later, Oefelein became a NASA astronaut and blasted off on the Space Shuttle Discovery for mission STS-116. His job was to help build the International Space Station, floating in zero gravity while constructing humanity's home among the stars. From a military base in Virginia to the endless black of space, this March birthday boy proved that dreams really can reach beyond the clouds.
activity_question: What was the name of the space shuttle mission that William Oefelein flew on?
bonus_fact: The International Space Station travels around Earth once every 90 minutes!

title: Boston's First Stanley Cup Victory
story: The Boston Bruins made hockey history on March 29th, 1929, when they defeated the New York Rangers 2-1 at Madison Square Garden. This victory gave Boston a 2-0 series sweep and their very first Stanley Cup championship! The crowd in New York watched as the visiting team celebrated on their home ice. For Boston fans, it was the moment they had been waiting for since the team started playing hockey.
activity_question: What was special about the Boston Bruins' 1929 Stanley Cup victory?
activity_choices: It was their first championship ever
They scored 10 goals in the game
activity_answer: It was their first championship ever
bonus_fact: The Bruins won the championship by playing only two games in the final series.

title: Cricket's Epic Six-Day Battle Begins
story: Two cricket teams stepped onto the field in Wellington, New Zealand on March 29th, 1946, ready to make history. Australia and New Zealand were about to play their very first official Test cricket match against each other! Test cricket is the longest form of the sport, where games can last up to five days. This wasn't just any game - it marked the beginning of a friendly rivalry that would last for decades, with both countries showing off their best players and strategies in this exciting new chapter of cricket history.
activity_question: How long can a Test cricket match last?
bonus_fact: New Zealand actually won this historic first Test match against Australia!

title: The Longest Spring Training Game Ever
story: Baseball fans got way more than they bargained for when the New York Yankees and Boston Red Sox faced off in spring training on this date back in 1948! What started as a regular practice game turned into an epic marathon that lasted 17 innings. Both teams kept scoring and then getting shut down, over and over again. Players were probably getting pretty tired by the 10th inning, but they kept playing. Finally, after what felt like forever, the game ended in a 2-2 tie. That's one way to get extra batting practice!
activity_question: How many innings did this epic spring training game last?
bonus_fact: Spring training games today rarely go past 9 innings, even if tied!

title: Strike! The Perfect Game Champion
story: Rick Steelsmith rolled his way into bowling history on this date in 1997 when he won the PBA National Championship! The Professional Bowlers Association tournament is like the Super Bowl for bowling pros. Steelsmith had to knock down thousands of pins and beat dozens of other skilled bowlers to claim the top prize. His victory proved that sometimes the coolest champions come from the most unexpected sports - and that perfect strikes can make you a legend!
activity_question: What does PBA stand for in bowling?
follow_up_question: What other sports have championship tournaments that most people don't know about?

title: Scott's Final Words from Antarctica
story: Captain Robert Falcon Scott was trapped in his tent somewhere near the bottom of the world, with howling Antarctic winds keeping him prisoner. On March 29th, 1912, this brave explorer picked up his pen one last time and wrote in his diary: "the end cannot be far." Scott and his team had reached the South Pole, but they discovered another team had beaten them there first. Now, on their way back, they were stuck in a terrible blizzard with no food left and no way to escape.
activity_question: What did Captain Scott write in his final diary entry?
activity_choices: "we made it to the South Pole"
bonus_fact: Scott's diary was found eight months later, still frozen in the tent.

title: Glass Pyramid Shocks Paris
story: A giant glass pyramid suddenly appeared in front of one of the world's most famous museums, and Parisians were not happy! On March 30th, 1989, architect I. M. Pei's bold new entrance to the Louvre Museum officially opened to the public. The shiny pyramid looked totally different from the old palace around it, and many people thought it was ugly and didn't belong. But Pei had a clever plan - the pyramid let natural light flood into the underground lobby below, making the museum much brighter and easier to visit.
activity_question: What did I. M. Pei's new entrance to the Louvre Museum look like?
bonus_fact: The pyramid is made of 603 pieces of glass and 70 pieces of steel.

title: America's First Male Gymnastics Gold Hero
story: Kurt Thomas flipped into history when he was born in Miami, Florida on this very date in 1956! This future gymnastics superstar would become the first American male ever to win a gold medal at the World Championships. In 1978, he dazzled judges with his floor routine, then came back in 1979 to win gold again on both floor and horizontal bars. But here's the twist - he also became a movie star in an action film called "Gymkata," where he used his incredible gymnastics skills to fight bad guys!
activity_question: What made Kurt Thomas special in gymnastics history?
activity_choices: He was the first American male to win World Championships gold
He invented a new type of gymnastics equipment
He was the tallest gymnast ever
He could do backflips while eating pizza
activity_answer: He was the first American male to win World Championships gold
bonus_fact: Kurt Thomas had a gymnastics move named after him - the "Thomas Flair" on pommel horse!

title: Greece's Golden Sprint to Glory
story: A future Olympic champion took her first breath in the small Greek village of Tripotamo on this very day in 1965. Voula Patoulidou was born that year with no idea she would one day make history for her entire country. Twenty-seven years later, she would sprint and leap over hurdles faster than any other woman in the world, winning Greece's first-ever Olympic gold medal in track and field at the 1992 Barcelona Olympics in the 100-meter hurdles!
activity_question: What Olympic event did Voula Patoulidou win gold in?
bonus_fact: Her victory was Greece's first Olympic track and field gold medal ever!

title: Ohio's Big Work Rule Change
story: Back on this date in 1852, Ohio did something pretty unusual - they made a new law saying kids under 18 and women couldn't work more than 10 hours a day. That might sound like a long time now, but back then, many people worked 12 or even 14 hours every single day! This was one of the first times a state said "Hey, that's too much work for some people." It was a big step toward making work safer and fairer, even though it would take many more years before work rules became truly equal for everyone.
activity_question: How many hours per day did Ohio's 1852 law limit work for children and women?
follow_up_question: Why do you think it took so long for work rules to become equal for everyone?

title: Giant Mirror's Epic Road Trip
story: A massive crowd of 10,000 people lined the streets of Indianapolis on this March day in 1936 to watch something incredible roll through their city. The biggest mirror blank ever made—a whopping 200 inches wide—was traveling by train to California to become the heart of a giant telescope. This wasn't just any piece of glass, but a 20-ton monster that would help scientists see farther into space than ever before!
activity_question: How many people watched the giant mirror blank pass through Indianapolis?
bonus_fact: The mirror blank weighed as much as 10 cars put together!

title: Beatles Finally Rock Russia
story: The Beatles' music was banned in Russia for over twenty years, but March 29th, 1986 changed everything! On this historic day, Russian fans could finally walk into stores and buy official Beatles records. For decades, people had to secretly trade bootleg copies or listen to fuzzy radio broadcasts from other countries. The government thought rock music was dangerous and would corrupt young minds. But by 1986, things were changing, and suddenly "Help!" and "Hey Jude" were spinning on turntables across the Soviet Union for the first time legally.
activity_question: How long were Beatles records banned in Russia before going on sale?
bonus_fact: Russian fans called bootleg Beatles records "music on bones" because they were pressed on old X-ray films.

title: When New York Played Against Itself
story: Baseball fans got a special treat on March 29th, 1996, when New York's two biggest teams faced off in an exhibition game. The Yankees and Mets don't usually play each other during the regular season, so this was like watching two siblings compete in the backyard. The Yankees won 7-3, but since it was just a practice game, both teams were really getting ready for the real season ahead.
activity_question: What was the final score when the Yankees beat the Mets in 1996?
bonus_fact: Exhibition games let teams practice against different opponents before the real season starts.

title: Mexico Buys Back Ancient Wonder
story: The Mexican government made a huge deal on March 29th, 2010 — they bought back one of the world's most famous ancient cities! For years, the incredible Mayan ruins of Chichen Itza belonged to a private landowner, even though millions of tourists visited to see the amazing pyramid and ball courts. The state of Yucatán finally purchased this archaeological treasure, which includes the famous El Castillo pyramid that's over 1,000 years old. Now this Wonder of the World truly belongs to the people of Mexico again!
activity_question: What famous ancient structure is located at Chichen Itza?
bonus_fact: Chichen Itza's pyramid has exactly 365 steps — one for each day of the year!

title: Love Wins in the UK
story: March 29th, 2014 became a celebration day across the United Kingdom when something historic happened - the very first same-sex couples got married! This special day was possible because of a new law called The Marriage (Same Sex Couples) Act that passed in 2013. For the first time ever, couples who had been together for years could finally have the same wedding rights as everyone else. Many couples had waited decades for this moment, and when that Saturday morning arrived, they rushed to make it official with happy tears and big smiles.
activity_question: What made March 29th, 2014 a historic day in the UK?
activity_choices: The first same-sex couples could legally marry
activity_answer: The first same-sex couples could legally marry
follow_up_question: Why do you think it was important for couples to have equal marriage rights?

title: The Redhead Who Shocked Basketball
story: Dave Cowens proved that heart beats height when he grabbed the NBA's Most Valuable Player award on this date in 1973. The Boston Celtics center stood just 6'9" — short for his position — but played like a giant among men. While other centers towered over him, Cowens used his fiery red hair and even fiercer determination to outwork everyone on the court, leading Boston to greatness and earning basketball's biggest individual honor.
activity_question: What made Dave Cowens unusual for an NBA center?
activity_choices: He was shorter than most centers
He played with his eyes closed
activity_answer: He was shorter than most centers
bonus_fact: Cowens was known for his incredible hustle and diving for loose balls despite being a center.

title: Brazil's First Capital Gets Born
story: Portuguese explorers picked the perfect spot along Brazil's coast and founded Salvador da Bahia on March 29th, 1549, making it the very first capital city of this huge South American country! The city's name means "Savior of the Bay," and it sat right next to a beautiful harbor where ships could safely dock. For over 200 years, this colorful city served as Brazil's most important place, welcoming traders, settlers, and adventurers from around the world before the capital eventually moved to Rio de Janeiro.
activity_question: What does Salvador da Bahia's name mean in English?
bonus_fact: Salvador da Bahia was Brazil's capital for 214 years!

title: Sweden's American Adventure Begins
story: Peter Minuit had a wild idea that would change history forever on March 29th, 1638! This brave explorer sailed across the dangerous Atlantic Ocean with the very first Swedish families to reach America. They landed on the Delaware Peninsula and founded New Sweden, creating the first Swedish colony in the New World. These tough pioneers brought their own language, customs, and dreams of a better life to a completely unknown land thousands of miles from home.
activity_question: What did Peter Minuit and the Swedish immigrants establish in 1638?
bonus_fact: Swedish colonists introduced log cabins to America, which became iconic frontier homes.

title: America's First Federal Highway Gets the Green Light
story: Congress made history on March 29th, 1806, when they said "yes" to building America's very first federal highway! The Great National Pike, which most people called the Cumberland Road, would stretch from Maryland all the way to Illinois. Before this amazing road, traveling across the country meant bumping along terrible dirt paths that turned into muddy messes when it rained. This new highway would change everything, making it much easier for families to move west and for goods to travel between states.
activity_question: What was the popular nickname for the Great National Pike?
bonus_fact: The road eventually stretched over 600 miles and cost about $7 million to build!

title: Soviet Hockey Stars Break the Ice
story: Hockey fans went wild when something amazing happened on March 29th, 1989! For the first time ever, Soviet hockey players got permission to play in the NHL. Before this day, these incredible athletes were stuck behind the Iron Curtain, unable to join North American teams. The Soviet Union had always kept their best players at home, but everything changed in 1989. This opened the door for legendary players to finally show their skills on NHL ice!
activity_question: What happened to Soviet hockey players for the first time in 1989?
activity_choices: They were allowed to play in the NHL
They invented a new type of hockey stick
They started playing hockey on roller skates
They formed their own league in Canada
activity_answer: They were allowed to play in the NHL
follow_up_question: Why do you think the Soviet Union finally decided to let their players join the NHL?

title: Ravens Rise from the Ashes
story: The Cleveland Browns were packing up and moving to Baltimore, but they needed a brand new name for their fresh start. On March 29th, 1996, team owners announced their choice: the Baltimore Ravens! The name came from Edgar Allan Poe's famous spooky poem "The Raven," since the writer had lived in Baltimore. Fans loved the connection to their city's literary history, and the Ravens were born.
activity_question: What inspired the name "Baltimore Ravens"?
bonus_fact: Edgar Allan Poe is buried in a Baltimore cemetery and lived there for several years.

title: Europe's Longest Bridge Opens in Portugal
story: Portugal made history on March 29th, 1998, when the massive Vasco da Gama Road bridge finally opened in Lisbon! This incredible bridge stretched longer than any other bridge in Europe at the time. Named after the famous explorer Vasco da Gama, it spans across the Tagus River and is so long that on foggy days, you can't see from one end to the other!
activity_question: What made the Vasco da Gama bridge special when it opened in 1998?
activity_choices: It was the longest bridge in Europe
It could fly like a plane
It was made entirely of gold
It was the tallest bridge in the world
activity_answer: It was the longest bridge in Europe
bonus_fact: The bridge is over 10 miles long - that's longer than 175 football fields!

title: The Cuban Boxing Legend's Birthday
story: March 29th, 1952 marked the birth of a future boxing superstar in Puerto Padre, Cuba. Teofilo Stevenson was born that day and would grow up to become one of the greatest amateur boxers ever. This heavyweight champion did something almost impossible - he won Olympic gold medals three times in a row! Stevenson claimed the top prize at the 1972, 1976, and 1980 Olympics, making him a legend in the boxing world and a hero in Cuba.
activity_question: How many Olympic gold medals did Teofilo Stevenson win in boxing?
bonus_fact: Stevenson turned down millions of dollars to stay an amateur boxer in Cuba.

title: The Tyler Rose Blooms
story: A future football legend took his first breath in Tyler, Texas on March 29th, 1955, when Earl Campbell was born. This baby would grow up to become one of the most powerful running backs ever to thunder across a football field! Campbell earned the nickname "The Tyler Rose" and proved he was special by winning the Heisman Trophy in 1977 at the University of Texas. He didn't stop there - he made it to five Pro Bowls and became a Hall of Fame player who could bulldoze through defenders like they were made of paper.
activity_question: What nickname did Earl Campbell earn?
bonus_fact: Campbell was so tough that defenders often got hurt trying to tackle him!

title: Sweden's Surprise American Colony
story: Swedish explorers sailed across the Atlantic Ocean and built Fort Christina on March 29th, 1638, creating Delaware's very first permanent white settlement! These brave Swedish Lutherans chose a spot along the Delaware River that would later become the city of Wilmington. It was pretty bold for Sweden to start a colony so far from home, competing with bigger countries like England and Spain for land in the New World.
activity_question: What country founded Fort Christina in 1638?
bonus_fact: The Swedish colony was called New Sweden and lasted about 17 years.

title: Cracking the Maya Code
story: A young Russian linguist named Yuri Knorosov stood before professors in Moscow on this day in 1955, defending his master's thesis about something incredible - he had figured out how to read ancient Maya writing! For hundreds of years, nobody could understand the mysterious symbols carved on Maya temples and monuments. Knorosov discovered that the symbols weren't just pictures, but actually represented sounds and syllables, like letters in our alphabet. His breakthrough opened up a whole lost world of Maya history and stories.
activity_question: What did Yuri Knorosov discover about Maya writing?
activity_choices: The symbols represented sounds and syllables
The symbols were just decorative art
The symbols were actually upside down
The symbols were a secret treasure map
activity_answer: The symbols represented sounds and syllables
bonus_fact: Knorosov never visited a Maya site but worked from books in a library!

title: Cricket's Closest Call Ever
story: March 29th, 1982 delivered one of cricket's most nail-biting finishes ever! In India's biggest cricket tournament, the Ranji Trophy, two teams battled it out with incredible skill. Delhi scored 707 runs in their first innings, while Karnataka managed 705 runs. Just two runs separated victory from defeat! Delhi won the championship by the tiniest possible margin, proving that in cricket, every single run counts.
activity_question: By how many runs did Delhi beat Karnataka to win the 1982 Ranji Trophy?
bonus_fact: The Ranji Trophy is named after a famous Indian prince who played cricket for England!

title: Ireland's Smoke-Free Revolution
story: Ireland shocked the world when it became the first country ever to ban smoking in all workplaces on March 29th, 2004. This meant no more cigarettes in offices, restaurants, or even pubs - which was huge news in Ireland where pubs are super important to social life! Many people thought it would never work, but Ireland proved that protecting workers' health was more important than old habits.
activity_question: What made Ireland's smoking ban in 2004 so special?
activity_choices: It was the first country to ban smoking in all workplaces
It only banned smoking in schools
activity_answer: It was the first country to ban smoking in all workplaces
follow_up_question: Why do you think other countries followed Ireland's example?

title: Beethoven's Big Break
story: A young musician named Ludwig van Beethoven stepped onto the stage at Vienna's fancy Burgtheater on March 29th, 1795, ready to prove himself as a pianist. The 24-year-old had been living in Vienna for just a few years, but this performance would change everything. His powerful playing and amazing talent wowed the audience that night, launching his career as one of history's greatest composers.
activity_question: How old was Beethoven when he had his debut performance in Vienna?
bonus_fact: Beethoven moved to Vienna to study with the famous composer Mozart, but Mozart died shortly after.

title: Jack Benny's Radio Debut
story: A comedian named Jack Benny stepped up to a microphone for the very first time on March 29th, 1932, appearing on Ed Sullivan's New York interview program. This moment launched one of radio's biggest stars! Benny would go on to make millions of people laugh with his funny timing and pretend stinginess, becoming so popular that families would gather around their radios just to hear his voice every week.
activity_question: What year did Jack Benny make his radio debut?
bonus_fact: Jack Benny always joked that he was 39 years old, no matter how old he really was!

title: The Man Who Started America's Oil Rush
story: Edwin Drake changed America forever when he was born in Greenville, New York on March 29th, 1819. This future businessman would grow up to drill the very first productive oil well in the United States! Before Drake's big discovery, people thought oil was just something that bubbled up from the ground naturally. But Drake figured out how to dig deep and pump it up on purpose, starting America's entire oil industry and changing how we power our world.
activity_question: What did Edwin Drake accomplish that changed America?
activity_choices: He drilled the first productive US oil well
He invented the first car engine
activity_answer: He drilled the first productive US oil well
bonus_fact: Drake's well was only 69 feet deep, but it launched a billion-dollar industry!

title: Broadway's Sparkling Pearl
story: Pearl Bailey sparkled into the world on March 29th, 1918, in Newport News, Virginia, and she was destined to become one of America's brightest stars! This amazing performer could sing, dance, and act so well that she won both a Tony Award and an Emmy Award. Pearl became famous for playing Dolly in the musical "Hello, Dolly!" on Broadway, where her powerful voice and joyful energy made audiences cheer night after night.
activity_question: What awards did Pearl Bailey win during her career?
bonus_fact: Pearl Bailey was also appointed as a special advisor to the United Nations!

title: The Pulsar Hunter's Birthday
story: A baby born in Philadelphia on this March day in 1941 would grow up to hunt for some of the weirdest objects in space! Joseph Hooton Taylor Jr. became an astrophysicist who discovered a brand new type of pulsar - spinning stars that shoot out beams of energy like cosmic lighthouses. His amazing find was so important that he won the Nobel Prize in Physics in 1993!
activity_question: What prize did Joseph Taylor win for discovering a new type of pulsar?
activity_choices: Academy Award for Best Space Movie
Grammy Award for Best Cosmic Sounds
Olympic Gold Medal in Star Hunting
bonus_fact: Pulsars spin incredibly fast - some rotate over 700 times per second!

title: The Comedian Who Cleans Houses
story: Amy Sedaris burst into the world on March 29th, 1961, in Endicott, New York, destined to become one of America's funniest people. This future comedy star would grow up to make millions laugh with her wild characters and silly voices. But here's the twist that makes Amy extra special: she's just as famous for her comedy as she is for her amazing cupcakes and cleaning tips! Amy proves you can be hilarious and helpful at the same time.
activity_question: What year was Amy Sedaris born?
bonus_fact: Amy once had a pet rabbit named Dusty who appeared on TV with her!

title: The Harmonica Hero's Big Day
story: John Popper blew into the world on March 29th, 1967, in Chardon, Ohio, and he would grow up to become one of the most amazing harmonica players ever! This future rock star didn't just play the harmonica like most people do - he turned it into a superpower, creating incredible sounds that helped make his band Blues Traveler famous with songs like "Run Around."
activity_question: What instrument did John Popper become famous for playing?
bonus_fact: John Popper often wore a special harmonica holder that let him play multiple harmonicas at once!

title: The Great Island Giveaway
story: Great Britain did something pretty unusual on May 2nd, 1864 - they gave away a bunch of beautiful islands! The Ionian Islands, including the famous vacation spot of Corfu, were handed back to Greece after being under British control for decades. This wasn't because of a war or anything dramatic - Britain just decided it was the right thing to do and wanted to help Greece become stronger as a country.
activity_question: What did Great Britain give back to Greece in 1864?
follow_up_question: Why do you think countries sometimes give territory back to other nations?

title: A New Kind of College Opens
story: March 29th, 1928 marked a special day for education in New York City when Yeshiva College received its official charter to operate as a real college. This wasn't just any school - it was the first college in America designed to let Jewish students study both regular subjects like math and science AND their religious traditions at the same time. Before this, students often had to choose one or the other, but Yeshiva College said "Why not both?" The school later grew so big and successful that it became Yeshiva University, proving that mixing different kinds of learning can create something amazing.
activity_question: What made Yeshiva College special when it opened in 1928?
activity_choices: It was the first college to combine religious and regular studies
It only taught math and science
It was the biggest college in America
It had classes taught by robots
activity_answer: It was the first college to combine religious and regular studies
follow_up_question: What subjects would you want to study together that don't usually go together?

title: Cricket's Ultimate Bowling Masterclass
story: March 29th, 1946 became one of cricket's most lopsided days when Australia demolished New Zealand for just 42 runs in Wellington. Bill O'Reilly and Ernie Toshack bowled like magicians, taking nine wickets between them and leaving the Kiwis stunned. This match also marked the debut of three future Australian legends who would dominate cricket for years to come.
activity_question: How many runs did New Zealand score in their disastrous innings?
bonus_fact: This remains one of the lowest Test cricket scores ever recorded in international play.

title: A Soccer Dream Takes Root
story: Kids in the Dutch town of Drachten had a big dream in 1948 - they wanted their own soccer team! On this day that year, the Drachtster Boys soccer club was officially born. The young players probably had no idea their team would still be kicking goals decades later. Starting a sports club might sound easy, but it takes real teamwork and dedication to make it last.
activity_question: In which country was the Drachtster Boys soccer team formed?
bonus_fact: Drachten is famous for removing all its traffic lights in a safety experiment!

title: The Gymnast Who Made History
story: Olga Tass was born in Pécs, Hungary on this date in 1929, and she would grow up to become one of the most amazing gymnasts ever! In 1956, she helped her Hungarian team win Olympic gold in something called "team portable apparatus" - a gymnastics event that doesn't even exist anymore. This special competition used equipment that could be moved around, making it totally different from today's gymnastics.
activity_question: What year was Olga Tass born?
bonus_fact: The portable apparatus event was so unique that it was only held at one Olympics!

title: Rock Star's Secret Name Swap
story: A baby named Peretz Bernstein was born in Queens, New York City on this date in 1959, but the world would know him by a completely different name! This little guy grew up to become Perry Farrell, the wild frontman of the rock band Jane's Addiction. He didn't just change his name - he changed his whole identity, becoming one of rock music's most creative and energetic performers, proving that sometimes reinventing yourself can lead to amazing things.
activity_question: What was Perry Farrell's real birth name?
follow_up_question: Why do you think some musicians choose to change their names when they become famous?

title: Vienna's Biggest Goodbye Ever
story: Vienna had never seen anything like it when Ludwig van Beethoven died on March 26th, 1827. Three days later, 20,000 people crowded the streets for his funeral — that's like filling a huge stadium! Even though Beethoven was completely deaf by then, his music had touched so many hearts that the entire city came to say goodbye to their beloved composer.
activity_question: How many people attended Beethoven's funeral in Vienna?
bonus_fact: Beethoven wrote his most famous symphonies after he went completely deaf.

title: The Sea Captain Who Saved Children
story: Thomas Coram sailed the seas as a ship captain, but his biggest adventure happened on land. Born in Lyme Regis on this very date back in 1668, he would grow up to become something amazing — a hero for children who had nowhere to go. Coram founded the Foundling Hospital, a safe place for kids without families.
activity_question: What did Thomas Coram create to help children?
bonus_fact: The Foundling Hospital was one of the first places in England to care for abandoned babies.

title: The Kora Master's Musical Magic
story: A baby boy named Mory Kanté was born on this day in 1950 in a small village in French Guinea, Africa. He would grow up to become famous around the world for playing the kora, a beautiful harp-like instrument with 21 strings. His biggest hit song was called "Yé ké yé ké," which means "it's all good" in his language!
activity_question: What instrument did Mory Kanté become famous for playing?
bonus_fact: The kora is made from a large gourd and sounds like a guitar and harp combined!

title: The Polka Dot Queen is Born
story: Yayoi Kusama was born in Matsumoto, Japan in 1929, and she would grow up to become one of the world's most famous artists! She's known for covering everything in colorful polka dots - rooms, sculptures, even giant pumpkins. Her art makes people feel like they're inside a magical, spotted dream world.
activity_question: What is Yayoi Kusama most famous for in her artwork?
bonus_fact: Kusama once covered a entire room with mirrors and polka dot lights!
